title: Genetics, Philadelphia Chromosome -- Development
content: The Philadelphia chromosome is generated when the proto-oncogene Abelson murine leukemia (ABL1)  gene, located on the band q34 on chromosome 9, translocates to the band q11 on chromosome 22 where the breakpoint cluster gene (BCR) is. [8] [12] The translocation t(9;22)(q34;q11) results in the formation of a fusion gene called BCR-ABL1. [4]

title: Genetics, Cytogenetic Testing and Conventional Karyotype -- Potential Diagnosis
content: Translocation between the long arms of chromosomes 9 and 22 t(9;22)(q34;q11) was the first demonstration that cancer could result from a genetic abnormality. This aberration was named the Philadelphia chromosome. [4] [17] It is key for the diagnosis of Chronic Myeloid Leukemia (CML), and may also occur in acute lymphoblastic leukemia (ALL) and acute myeloid leukemia (AML). This translocation results in the BCR-ABL fusion protein with constitutive tyrosine kinase activity. [4]

title: Genetics, Philadelphia Chromosome -- Biochemical

The Rho/GEF kinase domain plays a vital role in activating differentiation in the protooncogene induced leukemogenesis. [4] [13] The SH domain of ABL1 controls the gene’s activation and deactivation, and hence this domain is an important site for pharmacologic intervention. [4]

title: Chronic Myelogenous Leukemia -- Pathophysiology
content: The fusion oncoprotein BCR-ABL1 defines CML. 90% to 95% of patients with CML have a shortened chromosome 22 because of a reciprocal translocation t(9;22) (q34;q11.2) called the Philadelphia chromosome. The ABL1 gene encodes a non-receptor tyrosine kinase on chromosome 9, and BCR is a breakpoint cluster region on chromosome 22. The translated oncoprotein, in most cases, is 210-kd and called p210 BCR-ABL1 . Alternative splicing results in p190 and p230 BCR-ABL1 , which may show different presentations. This oncoprotein acts as a constitutively expressed defective tyrosine kinase. The downstream pathways affected include JAK/STAT, PI3K/AKT, and RAS/MEK; they involve cell growth, cell survival, inhibition of apoptosis, and activation of transcription factors. [5] The remainder of patients have variant or complex translocations involving additional chromosomes detected by routine cytogenetics or a cryptic BCR-ABL1 t ranslocation detected with fluorescent in situ hybridization (FISH) or reverse transcriptase-polymerase chain reaction (PCR). [6]

title: Genetics, Philadelphia Chromosome -- Introduction
content: Philadelphia chromosome (Ph), named after the city where it was first described, was described for the first time by Nowell and Hungerford in 1960, in two patients who had lost the long arm of chromosome 22. [1] [2] [3] As the Giemsa staining improved over the decade, Rowley showed that this truncated chromosome was generated as a result of the translocation of the long arm of chromosomes 9 and 22. [2] [3] [4] The presence of the abnormal Ph chromosome in cells of myeloid, megakaryocytic, along with erythroid lineages pointed to the origination of the disorder in a pluripotential stem cell. [2] Philadelphia chromosome is the hallmark of chronic myeloid leukemia (CML) along with some other leukemias including acute lymphoblastic leukemia (ALL) (mostly B cell ALL, rarely T cell ALL), acute myeloid leukemia (AML), chronic neutrophilic leukemia (CNL), and mixed phenotype acute leukemia (MPAL). [4] [5] [6] [7] [8] [9] [10] [11]

title: Genetics, Philadelphia Chromosome -- Clinical Significance
content: There have been reports of detection of the chromosomal abnormality rarely in normal healthy people. [2] Recently, a Philadelphia like ALL (Ph-like ALL) has been identified, which lacks the novel BCR-ABL1 translocation. [32] CML: The Ph chromosome is the diagnostic feature of CML. CML has an incidence of 50 per million per year and accounts for approximately 7 to 15% of the leukemias in the adult population. [2] [33] ALL: Philadelphia chromosome has been detected in 11 to 29 % of patients with ALL, although it is rarer with a prevalence of 1 to 3% in childhood ALL. [34] [35] AML: In patients with AML, the chromosomal abnormality has a reported incidence of less than 1.5%. [36]

title: Myeloproliferative Neoplasms -- Pathophysiology
content: Cytogenetics of CML is characterized by a reciprocal translocation between long arms of chromosomes 22 and 9, leading to the shortening of the length of the chromosome 22, also known as Philadelphia chromosomes, which is diagnostic of CML. The translocation involves the fusion of breakpoint cluster gene ( BCR ) on chromosome 22 with oncogene Abelson murine leukemia virus ( ABL ) on the long arm of chromosome 9 resulting in BCR-ABL fusion gene. The tyrosine kinase activity of the ABL oncogene is further enhanced with the fusion of BCR gene due to an encoding of a chimeric protein from BCR-ABL fusion gene. [5] In BCR-ABL1 negative MPNs, the restricted driver mutations are JAK2, myeloproliferative leukemia virus proto-oncogene (MPL), CALR, and CSF3R, which encodes for various tyrosine kinases.

title: Leukemia -- Introduction
content: Chronic myelogenous leukemia (CML): CML typically arises from reciprocal translocation and fusion of BCR on chromosome 22 and ABL1 on chromosome 9, resulting in dysregulated tyrosine kinase on chromosome 22 called the Philadelphia (Ph) chromosome. This, in turn, causes a monoclonal population of dysfunctional granulocytes, predominantly neutrophils, basophils, and eosinophils. [1] [3] [5] [6]

title: Genetics, Philadelphia Chromosome -- Clinical Significance -- THERAPEUTIC IMPLICATIONS:
content: In patients diagnosed with the chronic phase of CML, risk calculations are performed before initiation of therapy. For this purpose, two different scoring systems are used. [31] One is the Sokal risk calculation, which requires the age of the patient, platelet count, the percentage of myeloblasts found in the peripheral blood and size of the spleen, whereas the Hasford risk calculation utilizes the same variables as Sokal calculator except for the percentage of myeloblasts it uses the percentage of blasts, basophils, and eosinophils. [42] [43] [42] For patients that have an intermediate or higher risk score, the recommendation is to consider the second-generation TKIs as the first line. [30] [40] [41]

title: Genetics, Philadelphia Chromosome -- Clinical Significance -- THERAPEUTIC IMPLICATIONS:
content: For CML, at the moment, many (five FDA approved) tyrosine kinase inhibitors, belonging to different generations, (TKI) are being used for the treatment. TKIs belonging to different generations are currently available. Frist generation TKI includes imatinib, and second-generation TKI includes bosutinib, dasatinib, and nilotinib. Third generation TKIs include ponatinib. For the chronic phase, TKI first and second-generation TKIs are included in the frontline treatments recommended. [30] [38] [39] [40] [41]

title: Imatinib -- Indications
content: First-line pediatric Philadelphia chromosome-positive acute lymphoblastic leukemia (Ph+ ALL) in combination with chemotherapy AALL0031 Trial: treatment with imatinib plus chemotherapy resulted in 5-year disease-free survival of 70% compared to a 65% rate in the sibling donor bone-marrow transplant cohort. [6] Adult myelodysplastic (MDS)/myeloproliferative (MPD) diseases associated with platelet-derived growth factor receptor gene re-arrangements In an open-label phase 2 study, an additional study, and 12 published case report in adult myelodysplastic(MDS)/myeloproliferative(MPD) disease associated with platelet-derived growth factor receptor gene re-arrangements, imatinib induced a major cytogenetic response in 39% of patients and a complete hematologic response in 45% of patients.

title: Chronic Myelogenous Leukemia -- Prognosis
content: Cytogenetic analysis for additional abnormalities, in addition to the classic translocation, may be important for prognostic information. Previously, any additional chromosomal abnormalities were considered a risk for disease progression, tyrosine kinase inhibitor resistance, and worse prognosis. However, studies have shown that single additional chromosomal abnormalities, including trisomy 8, loss of Y, and an extra copy of the Philadelphia chromosome, do not impact survival. The presence of 2 or more additional chromosomal abnormalities or a single additional chromosomal abnormality involving i(17)(q10), the loss of 7 or deletion of 7q, and 3q26.2 rearrangements are poor prognostic indicators. [37]

title: Genetics, Philadelphia Chromosome -- Testing
content: Philadelphia chromosome is detectable with cytogenetic testing, or a reverse transcriptase PCR test can demonstrate the BCR/ABL transcripts. [2] Along with a quantitative PCR for BCR/ABL1, flow cytometry is also necessary to determine the lineage. As mentioned before, the patients usually have a concurrent genetic abnormality, so it is reasonable to get a mutational analysis done. [30] [31] In addition to the above testing, liver function tests, as well as renal function tests, should be performed. [30]

title: Genetics, Philadelphia Chromosome -- Biochemical
content: As a result of different breakpoints in the BCR gene, that occur over quite a short DNA stretch of 5 -6 kilobases in the middle of the gene, three different BCR/ABL1 proteins are formed namely P190, P210, P230 which are usually associated with ALL, CML and CNL respectively. The numeral value represents the kDa size of the hybrid protein. There have been some reports showing correlations of P190 with CML, P210 with ALL, and P190 or P210 with AML. P230is formed by the fusion of the ABL1 gene with almost the entire BCR gene leading to the production of a 230-kDa protein. [2] It is the molecular diagnostic marker for neutrophilic-chronic myeloid leukemia (CML-N). [4] [9]

title: Genetics, Philadelphia Chromosome -- Clinical Significance -- PROGNOSTIC SIGNIFICANCE:
content: In patients of CML, the location of the breakpoint in the BCR gene has shown an important association with prognosis. CML patients with a double Philadelphia chromosome are associated with a worse prognosis than those with a single Philadelphia chromosome. [46] The presence of concurrent genetic abnormalities is associated with a worse prognosis than in the blastic phase of CML. [2] ALL patients, both adults, as well as children who are Ph+ive, have shown a poor prognosis in comparison to those who are Ph -ive. [8] Patients with Ph+ive MPAL have shown a worse outcome in comparison to other leukemic phenotypes. [4]

title: Laboratory Evaluation of Acute Leukemia -- Diagnostic Tests -- Cytogenetic Analysis
content: Therapeutic implications of cytogenetic analysis : Cytogenetic analysis provides valuable prognostic information and directly influences treatment decisions. Identifying specific chromosomal abnormalities enables targeted therapies that can improve patient outcomes. Philadelphia chromosome–positive acute lymphoblastic leukemia: Patients with the t(9;22) translocation benefit from tyrosine kinase inhibitors (TKIs), such as imatinib or dasatinib, in combination with chemotherapy. [50] Acute promyelocytic leukemia: The t(15;17) translocation in APL leads to the fusion of the PML and RARA genes. This subtype is highly responsive to all-trans retinoic acid and arsenic trioxide, resulting in high cure rates. [51]

CML affects both the peripheral blood and the bone marrow.

title: Myeloproliferative Neoplasms -- Treatment / Management
content: Chronic myeloid leukemia (CML) : Philadelphia chromosome is the BCR-ABL1 fusion gene which results in dysregulated tyrosine kinase activity. Tyrosine kinase inhibitors (TKI) targets tyrosine kinase activity of the BCR-ABL1 fusion gene. CML is divided into three phases: chronic phase accelerated phase and blast phase. Patients with chronic phase are managed initially with TKIs such as imatinib (first-generation TKIs) or nilotinib and dasatinib (second-generation TKIs). Based on ENESTND and DASISION, Nilotionib and Dasatinib respectively were superior to Imatinib in terms of achieving cytogenetic and molecular remission.  Patients in accelerated phase are treated with second-generation TKI and depending upon response may need hematopoietic stem cell transplantation (HCT). However blast phase CML patients undergo treatment with TKI and are evaluated for stem cell transplant.

Osteopontin (OPN), which overexpresses in BCR/ABL1, uses this pathway to maintain a leukemic microenvironment. It, in turn, promotes and maintains the proliferation and survival of leukemic cells as well as plays a role in cell differentiation and apoptosis. [4] [18]

title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1286
content: Imatinib is a BCR‑ABL inhibitor, meaning it belongs to the class of drugs that specifically target BCR‑ABL fusion proteins. BCR‑ABL fusion proteins serve as the molecular target for BCR‑ABL inhibitors; they are oncogenic drivers created by the Philadelphia chromosome translocation and are central to the pathogenesis of chronic myeloid leukemia and certain acute lymphoblastic leukemias. By binding to and inhibiting these fusion proteins, imatinib and other BCR‑ABL inhibitors can suppress the abnormal signaling that promotes uncontrolled cell proliferation, thereby providing an effective targeted therapy for patients with these malignancies.

title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 3 -- Community 2646
content: Dasatinib and dasatinib anhydrous are both classified as BCR‑ABL inhibitors, a class of targeted therapies that block the BCR‑ABL tyrosine kinase activity implicated in certain leukemias. By inhibiting this oncogenic kinase, both the standard and anhydrous formulations of dasatinib help to control malignant cell proliferation and are used in the management of chronic myeloid leukemia and Philadelphia chromosome‑positive acute lymphoblastic leukemia. Recognizing dasatinib and its anhydrous form as BCR‑ABL inhibitors underscores their shared mechanism of action and therapeutic role in precision oncology.

title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 4 -- Community 2916
content: Dasatinib anhydrous and imatinib are both classified as BCR‑ABL inhibitors. As BCR‑ABL inhibitors, these agents specifically target the BCR‑ABL fusion protein, a constitutively active tyrosine kinase that drives the development of chronic myeloid leukemia and certain acute lymphoblastic leukemias. By inhibiting BCR‑ABL activity, dasatinib anhydrous and imatinib disrupt downstream signaling pathways, reducing malignant cell proliferation and providing therapeutic benefit in BCR‑ABL–positive hematologic malignancies. Their shared classification highlights a common mechanism of action, while differences in potency, spectrum of activity, and side‑effect profiles inform clinicians’ choices of targeted therapy for patients with BCR‑ABL–driven disease.

title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 326
content: Ponatinib, ponatinib hydrochloride, and asciminib are therapeutic agents that may be used to treat chronic myeloid leukemia (ابيضاض الدم النقوي). Both ponatinib and its hydrochloride salt share the same indication, reflecting that the active compound is effective in this hematologic malignancy. Asciminib, a distinct BCR‑ABL1 inhibitor, also offers a treatment option for patients with chronic myeloid leukemia, particularly those with resistance to earlier tyrosine‑kinase inhibitors. The availability of multiple agents targeting the same disease provides clinicians with alternative strategies to achieve disease control and improve patient outcomes.

title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 4 -- Community 2801
content: CTRP Biomarker Terminology is a specialized classification system that incorporates the concept of bcr-abl fusion proteins as well as the concept of glukokortikoidy (glucocorticoids). By including bcr-abl fusion proteins, the terminology acknowledges the relevance of these oncogenic fusion proteins—commonly associated with chronic myeloid leukemia and other hematologic malignancies—as important biomarkers for disease detection, prognosis, and targeted therapy. The inclusion of glukokortikoidy reflects the significance of glucocorticoids, a class of steroid hormones that are widely used in clinical practice for their anti‑inflammatory and immunosuppressive effects and can also serve as biomarkers for therapeutic response and endocrine function. Together, these concepts illustrate the breadth of CTRP Biomarker Terminology in covering both molecular oncogenic markers and pharmacologically active hormone classes relevant to patient diagnosis and management.

content: CTRP Biomarker Terminology is a classification framework that encompasses a diverse set of biomarker concepts, including the bcr‑abl fusion proteins, human factor VIII, and the sex steroid hormones. By grouping these distinct molecular entities—oncogenic fusion proteins (bcr‑abl), a coagulation factor critical for hemostasis (factor VIII), and endogenous steroid hormones that regulate reproductive and metabolic functions—CTRP Biomarker Terminology provides a structured reference for researchers and clinicians to identify and discuss biomarkers across oncology, hematology, and endocrinology. This inclusive categorization facilitates interdisciplinary communication and supports integrated approaches to diagnosis, monitoring, and therapeutic decision‑making that involve these varied biomarker types.

title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 1 -- Community 48
content: BCR‑ABL inhibitors, a subgroup of protein kinase inhibitors classified as antineoplastic and immunomodulating agents, target the BCR‑ABL fusion protein. Dasatinib, dasatinib anhydrous and imatinib are BCR‑ABL inhibitors indicated for lymphoid leukemia, whereas ponatinib, ponatinib hydrochloride and asciminib are BCR‑ABL inhibitors used for myeloid leukemia. Bruton’s tyrosine kinase (BTK) inhibitors, also protein kinase inhibitors with antineoplastic and immunomodulating activity, include ibrutinib and zanubrutinib; ibrutinib is approved for chronic lymphocytic leukemia. Epidermal growth factor receptor (EGFR) inhibitors, another subclass of protein kinase inhibitors, comprise canertinib dihydrochloride and margetuximab, both belonging to the same antineoplastic and immunomodulating class. Cabozantinib, gilteritinib and midostaurin are members of the ATC subgroup “Other protein kinase inhibitors,” also classified as antineoplastic and immunomodulating agents, and gilteritinib and midostaurin have activity against acute myeloid leukemia. Collectively, these agents illustrate how precise molecular targeting of BCR‑ABL, BTK, EGFR and other kinases provides distinct therapeutic options across lymphoid, myeloid and chronic lymphocytic leukemias, underscoring the role of targeted kinase inhibition in modern oncology care.

title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 1 -- Community 98
content: The community’s knowledge base defines leukemia (ابْيضَاضُ الدَّم) as a hematologic malignancy comprising lymphoid leukemia (ابيضاض الدم اللمفاوي) and myeloid leukemia (ابيضاض الدم النقوي). BCR‑ABL inhibitors—dasatinib, dasatinib anhydrous, imatinib, ponatinib, ponatinib hydrochloride, and asciminib—share the mechanism of inhibiting the BCR‑ABL tyrosine‑kinase fusion protein and are assigned to specific subtypes: dasatinib, its anhydrous form, and imatinib are indicated for lymphoid leukemia, whereas ponatinib, ponatinib hydrochloride, and asciminib are indicated for myeloid leukemia. Additional anti‑leukemic agents include the corticosteroid derivative kortizon acetát, dexamethasone phosphate, the topoisomerase‑I inhibitor irinotecan hydrochloride, the antimetabolite methotrexate sodium, the alkylating agent pipobroman, and several prednisolone salts (prednisolone sodium metasulphobenzoate, prednisolone sodium phosphate, prednisolone tebutate). Conversely, the oncolytic viral therapy talimogene laherparepvec is contraindicated in patients with any form of leukemia. These relationships support precise drug selection and comprehensive management of hematologic oncology patients.

title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 1 -- Community 70
content: BCR‑ABL inhibitors, a subclass of protein kinase inhibitors with antineoplastic and immunomodulating properties, include dasatinib, dasatinib anhydrous, imatinib, ponatinib, ponatinib hydrochloride and asciminib. Dasatinib, dasatinib anhydrous and imatinib are indicated for lymphoid leukemia, whereas ponatinib, ponatinib hydrochloride and asciminib are indicated for myeloid leukemia. Bruton's tyrosine kinase (BTK) inhibitors, also protein kinase inhibitors with antineoplastic and immunomodulating activity, comprise ibrutinib and zanubrutinib; ibrutinib is used to treat chronic lymphocytic leukemia. Epidermal growth factor receptor (EGFR) inhibitors, another protein kinase inhibitor subclass, consist of canertinib dihydrochloride and margetuximab. By blocking EGFR tyrosine‑kinase signaling, these agents disrupt tumor cell proliferation and survival and can modulate immune responses, providing antineoplastic and immunomodulating effects. Collectively, targeted inhibition of BCR‑ABL, BTK, and EGFR exemplifies precision oncology, linking specific molecular targets to distinct hematologic and solid malignancies and guiding patient‑centered cancer therapy.

title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 1 -- Community 81
content: Blood cancer (ابْيضَاضُ الدَّم) encompasses leukemias, including lymphoid (ابيضاض الدم اللمفاوي) and myeloid (ابيضاض الدم النقوي) forms, with specific subtypes such as chronic lymphocytic leukemia (سرطان الخلايا الليمفاوية المزمن) and acute myeloid leukemia (سرطان الدم النخاعي الحاد). Targeted kinase inhibition is a central therapeutic strategy. BCR‑ABL inhibitors—dasatinib, dasatinib anhydrous, imatinib, ponatinib, ponatinib hydrochloride, and asciminib—are employed, the first three for lymphoid leukemia and the latter three for myeloid leukemia. BTK inhibitors, namely ibrutinib and zanubrutinib, belong to the same protein‑kinase inhibitor class, with ibrutinib indicated for chronic lymphocytic leukemia. Additional protein‑kinase inhibitors with antineoplastic and immunomodulating activity include EGFR inhibitors canertinib dihydrochloride and margetuximab, as well as cabozantinib, gilteritinib, and midostaurin; gilteritinib and midostaurin are specifically used in acute myeloid leukemia. Conventional cytotoxic and corticosteroid agents such as cytarabine, dexamethasone acetate, dexamethasone phosphate, dexamethasonisonikotinát, irinotecan hydrochloride, pipobroman, and prednisolone sodium metasulphobenzoate, prednisolone sodium phosphate, and prednisolone tebutate also have activity against various blood cancers. Dexamethasone acetate additionally serves in the management of septic shock. Conversely, viral agents such as human herpesvirus‑1 and the oncolytic therapy talimogene laherparepvec are contraindicated in patients with blood cancer. These interrelated disease classifications, drug classes, and contraindications illustrate the complexity of therapeutic decision‑making in hematologic malignancies.

A: yes

Bruton’s tyrosine kinase (BTK) inhibitors, ibrutinib and zanubrutinib, also belong to this class, with ibrutinib specifically used for chronic lymphocytic leukemia.
